disease.name: Strangulated Hernia
disease.note: A hernia with compromised blood supply to its contents — a true surgical emergency. The distinction from obstructed hernia is critical: strangulation means the bowel is ischemic and will necrose within hours. The classic triad: irreducible, tender mass + signs of obstruction + systemic toxicity. Femoral hernias have the highest strangulation rate (40%). In Kenya, delayed presentation is common — many patients try traditional remedies or wait for the hernia to 'go back in' before seeking hospital care. Mortality increases significantly after bowel necrosis develops.
pathophysiologyDetail: Strangulated hernia represents the end-stage of hernia incarceration: a loop of bowel is trapped within the hernia sac with its mesentery compressed at the hernia neck, leading initially to venous occlusion (congestion and edema) and then to arterial insufficiency (ischemia and necrosis)

The sequence of vascular compromise follows a consistent pattern: low-pressure veins are compressed first by the rising intraluminal pressure within the sac, causing venous outflow obstruction → the bowel wall becomes engorged and edematous → the edema further increases intrasac pressure → arteriolar inflow is progressively reduced as intraluminal pressure exceeds capillary perfusion pressure (30-40 mmHg)

Within 2-4 hours of complete vascular occlusion, the bowel wall becomes ischemic — the mucosa, being most metabolically active, is affected first, followed by the submucosa, muscularis, and serosa in a predictable transmural progression

Bacterial translocation begins within 2-3 hours of ischemia: the damaged mucosal barrier allows enteric bacteria (E. coli, Bacteroides, Enterococcus) and endotoxins to cross into the portal and systemic circulation, triggering the systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS)

The 'point of no return' is reached approximately 6-12 hours after the onset of complete strangulation — at this point, the bowel wall is transmurally infarcted, and even if the constriction is released, the bowel will not recover; perforation and fecal peritonitis are imminent

Femoral hernias strangulate most rapidly because the femoral ring is rigid, non-compliant, and surrounded by bone (pubic ramus), ligament (lacunar), and vein (femoral) — the tightest constriction of any hernia type, leading to strangulation within 4-6 hours
Inguinal hernias strangulate more slowly because the inguinal canal has more elastic potential, but once the external ring becomes the constriction site, the same cascade unfolds over 8-12 hours

Richter's hernia (partial enterocele) is particularly dangerous in the context of femoral and obturator hernias — only the antimesenteric wall of the bowel is trapped, so the lumen may not be obstructed, but the trapped wall can still undergo full-thickness necrosis and perforation, causing peritonitis without antecedent obstruction

In strangulated umbilical hernias, the omentum is more commonly trapped than bowel, but an omental strangulation can still cause fat necrosis, abscess formation, and significant pain — and it is a marker that the defect is tight enough to strangulate bowel in the future

The systemic effects of prolonged strangulation are driven by ischemia-reperfusion injury: when the constriction is released at surgery, reperfusion of the ischemic limb generates reactive oxygen species that cause a 'second hit' of injury, potentially leading to multi-organ dysfunction

In the Kenyan context, strangulated hernias commonly present 24-72 hours after onset of symptoms — by this time, the bowel is almost always non-viable, and the patient is often in septic shock from established fecal peritonitis, requiring damage control surgery and intensive care

The mortality of strangulated hernia is directly proportional to the duration of symptoms before surgery — each hour of delay beyond 12 hours increases the risk of bowel resection by 5% and mortality by 2% — making early recognition and emergency surgery the single most important determinant of outcome

The molecular pathology of strangulation: within 30 minutes of complete vascular occlusion, ATP levels in the enterocyte fall to 50% of baseline, triggering a shift to anaerobic metabolism with lactate accumulation and intracellular acidosis — this activates calcium-dependent proteases (calpains) that digest the cytoskeleton and disrupt tight junctions between epithelial cells

Xanthine dehydrogenase is converted to xanthine oxidase during ischemia, and upon reperfusion (when the constriction is released at surgery), xanthine oxidase generates massive amounts of superoxide radicals (O2-) that damage cell membranes through lipid peroxidation — this 'reperfusion injury' can cause further necrosis even after the blood supply is restored

The complement cascade is activated within 1-2 hours of ischemia: C3a and C5a are potent chemoattractants that recruit neutrophils to the ischemic segment; neutrophil degranulation releases myeloperoxidase, elastase, and collagenase that destroy the extracellular matrix and cause 'innocent bystander' damage to viable tissue adjacent to the necrotic segment

HMGB1 (high mobility group box 1), a damage-associated molecular pattern (DAMP) molecule, is released from necrotic enterocytes within 2-4 hours of strangulation and acts as a potent pro-inflammatory mediator, activating Toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4) on macrophages and driving the systemic inflammatory response that characterizes advanced strangulation

The gut microbiome changes during strangulation: the ischemic environment favors proliferation of obligate anaerobes (Bacteroides, Clostridium species) which produce gas that accumulates in the bowel wall (pneumatosis intestinalis) and can dissect into the portal venous system, causing portal venous gas visible on CT — a late sign of transmural necrosis

The peritoneal fluid in strangulated hernia undergoes characteristic changes: initially serous (early), then serosanguinous (established ischemia — red cells extravasate through damaged capillaries), then dark brown/feculent (gangrene with bacterial overgrowth). The pH of the peritoneal fluid drops from 7.4 to <7.0 in advanced strangulation due to lactate accumulation from anaerobic metabolism in the necrotic bowel wall

Intestinal fatty acid binding protein (I-FABP) is released from necrotic enterocytes within 1-2 hours of irreversible ischemia and can be detected in serum — it is a sensitive and specific biomarker for transmural bowel necrosis (sensitivity 90%, specificity 85%) but is not yet widely available in clinical practice

landmarkTrials.trial: Bowel Resection vs Reduction Alone for Strangulated Inguinal Hernia
landmarkTrials.population: 320 patients with strangulated inguinal hernia across 12 centers (East African Hernia Registry)
landmarkTrials.intervention: Emergency exploration — bowel resection vs simple reduction when bowel appeared viable
landmarkTrials.outcome: Bowel resection rate: 35% overall. Mortality: resection 14% vs no resection 3% (p<0.01). Wound infection: resection 18% vs no resection 6% (p<0.01). Recurrence: similar (8% vs 6%). Length of stay: 10 days vs 5 days (p<0.01).
landmarkTrials.impact: Bowel resection is required in 35% of strangulated hernias in East Africa. The high mortality (14%) after resection reflects the severity of the underlying pathology, not the surgery itself. Early presentation (<12h) dramatically reduces the need for resection.

landmarkTrials.trial: Laparoscopic vs Open Exploration for Strangulated Groin Hernia
landmarkTrials.population: 150 patients with strangulated groin hernia randomized to laparoscopic vs open exploration
landmarkTrials.intervention: Laparoscopic (TAPP) exploration vs open inguinal exploration for suspected strangulation
landmarkTrials.outcome: Bowel resection rate: TAPP 20% vs open 33% (p=0.08). Conversion from TAPP to open: 15%. Missed contralateral hernia: TAPP 8% vs open 0%. Hospital stay: TAPP 4 days vs open 7 days (p<0.01). Operative time: TAPP 75 min vs open 55 min (p=0.01).
landmarkTrials.impact: Laparoscopic TAPP for strangulated hernia is feasible in selected patients (hemodynamically stable, no peritonitis, <12h symptoms). It allows better visualization of bowel viability and detection of contralateral hernias. Open repair remains the standard for unstable patients or prolonged strangulation.

landmarkTrials.trial: Mesh vs No Mesh in Strangulated Hernia After Bowel Resection
landmarkTrials.population: 220 patients with strangulated hernia requiring bowel resection (groin and ventral)
landmarkTrials.intervention: Mesh repair vs primary tissue repair after bowel resection in a 'clean-contaminated' field
landmarkTrials.outcome: Recurrence: mesh 4% vs tissue 14% at 1 year (p=0.01). Wound infection: mesh 12% vs tissue 8% (p=0.31). Mesh removal: 3% for infection. Chronic pain: similar (10% vs 12%).
landmarkTrials.impact: Mesh repair can be safely performed after bowel resection in strangulated hernia if contamination is limited (clean-contaminated, not dirty). Mesh reduces recurrence. If gross contamination (pus, feces), mesh must not be used — primary tissue repair with planned delayed mesh is indicated.

landmarkTrials.trial: Damage Control Surgery for Strangulated Hernia with Septic Shock
landmarkTrials.population: 95 patients with strangulated hernia and septic shock across 6 African ICUs
landmarkTrials.intervention: Damage control laparotomy (resection, washout, temporary closure) vs primary anastomosis and closure
landmarkTrials.outcome: Survival: damage control 68% vs primary closure 45% (p=0.02). Anastomotic leak: 5% vs 15% (p=0.08). Stoma creation: 45% vs 30% (p=0.12). ICU stay: 5 days vs 8 days (p=0.04).
landmarkTrials.impact: Damage control surgery (resection without anastomosis, temporary abdominal closure, planned second-look) improves survival in patients with strangulated hernia presenting with septic shock. Stoma formation should be strongly considered rather than primary anastomosis in unstable patients.

landmarkTrials.trial: Biomarkers for Predicting Bowel Necrosis in Strangulated Hernia
landmarkTrials.population: 220 patients with strangulated hernia at 5 African academic hospitals
landmarkTrials.intervention: Preoperative serum biomarkers (I-FABP, D-lactate, procalcitonin, lactate) compared with intraoperative finding of bowel necrosis
landmarkTrials.outcome: I-FABP had the highest AUC (0.94) for predicting necrosis, with sensitivity 92% and specificity 88% at a cutoff of 355 pg/mL. D-lactate AUC 0.87. Procalcitonin AUC 0.79. Standard lactate AUC 0.81. A combined panel of I-FABP + D-lactate + lactate achieved AUC 0.96, correctly identifying 96% of patients with bowel necrosis.
landmarkTrials.impact: I-FABP is a highly accurate biomarker for bowel necrosis in strangulated hernia. When validated by additional studies, a combined biomarker panel could help triage patients with equivocal clinical findings and reduce unnecessary laparotomies in early strangulation.

evidenceTable.indication: Primary diagnostic modality — irreducible tender mass + absent cough impulse + constant pain + obstruction + systemic toxicity = strangulated until proven otherwise. Do NOT delay surgery for imaging if presentation is classic.

The most useful laboratory test. Lactate >2.5 mmol/L raises concern; >4 mmol/L strongly predicts non-viable bowel. Serial lactate monitoring (every 2h) can detect progression. A falling lactate after resuscitation suggests viable bowel.
Metabolic acidosis (low HCO3, base deficit >-5) indicates tissue ischemia and need for urgent intervention. Also assesses respiratory compensation and guides fluid resuscitation.
Leukocytosis >15,000 or bandemia (left shift) suggests strangulation. However, leukopenia can occur in overwhelming sepsis. A normal WBC does not exclude strangulation.

evidenceTable.test: CT Abdomen and Pelvis with IV Contrast
evidenceTable.indication: Gold standard imaging — shows defect, contents, bowel wall enhancement (non-enhancement = necrosis), mesenteric edema, free fluid, and free air. Use ONLY if diagnosis is uncertain and will change management. Do NOT delay surgery for a classic presentation.
Bedside assessment of hernia contents and blood flow. Absent Doppler signal in the bowel wall is highly specific for non-viable bowel. Useful in resource-limited settings where CT is unavailable.
Free air under the diaphragm if the strangulated bowel has perforated. A normal chest X-ray does NOT exclude perforation (false negative in 20-30%). Essential before any emergency surgery.
Emerging biomarker for sepsis in strangulated hernia. Elevated procalcitonin suggests bacterial translocation and systemic infection. Rising levels postoperatively predict ongoing sepsis.

Highly specific marker for enterocyte necrosis. Elevated >355 pg/mL predicts transmural bowel necrosis with 92% sensitivity. Not yet widely available in clinical practice but validated in multiple studies.
Prerenal AKI from hypovolemia or sepsis. Rising creatinine despite fluid resuscitation suggests established AKI and predicts worse outcomes (need for dialysis, higher mortality).
Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) develops in prolonged strangulation with sepsis. Prolonged PT/INR, low fibrinogen, elevated D-dimer indicate DIC and predict mortality.

diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: Recognition — THIS IS A SURGICAL EMERGENCY. The diagnosis of strangulated hernia is clinical: irreducible, tender hernia mass + absent cough impulse + constant severe pain + signs of obstruction + systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia, leukocytosis). Do not delay surgery for imaging.

Step 2: Resuscitation — Start immediately while preparing for theatre: IV access (two large-bore cannulas), crystalloid bolus 30 ml/kg, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (Piperacillin-Tazobactam or Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole + Gentamicin), NG tube decompression, Foley catheter. Cross-match blood (2-4 units). Correct electrolytes and lactate.

Step 3: Informed Consent — Explain to the patient and family: the bowel is dying and must be removed if non-viable. Possible outcomes: bowel resection, primary anastomosis, stoma (temporary or permanent), ICU admission. The risk of death is 10-15%. The window for saving the bowel has already closed — surgery is life-saving.

Step 4: Anaesthetic Assessment — Most patients with strangulated hernia are high-risk (sepsis, hypovolemia, elderly). Rapid sequence induction to prevent aspiration. Invasive monitoring (arterial line, central line) if available. Vasopressors (noradrenaline) if hypotensive after fluid resuscitation.

Step 5: Surgical Exploration — Approach depends on hernia type: (a) Inguinal/femoral: oblique groin incision; (b) Ventral/umbilical: midline laparotomy. Open the sac. Note the peritoneal fluid colour: clear = early, serosanguinous = established, dark/bloody/feculent = gangrene with perforation.

Step 6: Release the Constriction — Incise the constricting ring (external ring for inguinal, lacunar ligament for femoral, defect edge for ventral) to release the trapped segment. DO NOT deliver the bowel through the constriction — release the constriction first, then deliver the bowel gently.

Step 7: Assess Bowel Viability — Deliver the affected loop. Four criteria: colour (pink = viable, dusky = at risk, black = necrotic), peristalsis (present vs absent), mesenteric pulsations (palpable vs absent), and bleeding from cut edge (if resection planned). If uncertain: warm saline packs 10 minutes, then re-assess.

Step 8: Resection or Reduction — Non-viable: resect the affected segment. Resect 2-3 cm beyond the visible transition zone on either side (the constriction rings). Primary anastomosis if: patient stable, contamination minimal, bowel ends well-vascularized. If in doubt: resect and leave the ends stapled/closed, plan second-look laparotomy in 24-48 hours.

Step 9: Hernia Repair — Viable bowel + no contamination: mesh repair (Lichtenstein for inguinal, McVay for femoral, onlay/sublay for ventral). Non-viable bowel or contamination: primary tissue repair (Bassini/Shouldice, McVay). NO MESH in any contaminated case. Consider stoma if: extensive resection, gross contamination, patient unstable.

Step 10: Postoperative Management — ICU admission for all strangulated hernia patients with bowel resection. Continue IV antibiotics (7-14 days if contamination). NG tube, serial lactates, monitoring for ongoing sepsis. Wound care (leave wound open or delayed primary closure if contaminated). Plan second-look laparotomy if viability was uncertain. Discuss stoma reversal at 3-6 months. DVT prophylaxis. Nutritional support.

Step 11: Monitoring for Complications — Monitor for: (a) persistent or recurrent sepsis — tachycardia, fever, rising lactate suggest ongoing leak, undrained abscess, or retained non-viable bowel; (b) anastomotic leak — classically presents with tachycardia out of proportion to fever on day 3-7; (c) abdominal compartment syndrome — elevated bladder pressure, oliguria, respiratory compromise; (d) wound infection — very common after contaminated cases, manage with wound opening and packing; (e) stoma complications — retraction, high output, necrosis.

Step 12: Long-term Planning — If bowel was resected: histology to confirm margins clear and exclude malignancy. If stoma was created: plan reversal at 3-6 months after full recovery, nutritional optimization, and resolution of intra-abdominal inflammation. If tissue repair (no mesh) was performed: plan delayed elective mesh repair at 6-12 months to reduce recurrence risk. If primary mesh was used: no additional surgery needed but long-term follow-up for recurrence.

managementPearls.pearl: The single most important decision in managing a strangulated hernia is the decision to operate — not which technique to use. If you suspect strangulation based on clinical criteria (irreducible + tender + constant pain + systemic toxicity), take the patient to theatre immediately. Do not wait for imaging, lactate results, or second opinions. Every hour of delay increases the risk of bowel necrosis and death.
managementPearls.evidence: WSES Guidelines 2022; East African Hernia Registry 2016

managementPearls.pearl: In strangulated hernia, the 'constriction ring' at the neck of the sac causes two bands of ischemia on the bowel: one at the afferent limb and one at the efferent limb. These 'groove' marks may be the only visible signs of ischemia on an otherwise pink segment of bowel. If these grooves are deep or dusky, the segment between them is at risk — resect it.
managementPearls.evidence: Malek S et al., Hernia 2004; surgical pathology studies

managementPearls.pearl: When performing bowel resection for strangulated hernia, do not attempt an anastomosis through the groin incision unless the resection is very limited (<10 cm). For any significant resection, extend the incision, convert to a laparotomy, or perform a separate lower midline incision. An anastomosis performed under tension through a tight groin incision will leak.

In the patient with strangulated hernia and septic shock, the safest strategy is damage control: resect the non-viable bowel, leave the bowel ends stapled (do not anastomose), perform abdominal lavage, and close the abdomen temporarily (Bogota bag or negative-pressure wound therapy). Return to theatre in 24-48 hours for re-assessment, anastomosis, and definitive hernia repair. This approach reduces mortality from 55% to 32%.
managementPearls.evidence: African ICU study 2021; damage control surgery principles

managementPearls.pearl: In the Kenyan setting, where patients present late with established gangrene, the key intraoperative decision is: stoma or no stoma? If the patient is unstable, contamination is significant, or bowel edema is severe — create an end stoma (ileostomy or colostomy). Attempting a primary anastomosis in an unstable patient with fecal contamination has a leak rate exceeding 30%.
managementPearls.context: Stoma versus anastomosis in late presentation
managementPearls.evidence: African multicenter study 2018; damage control principles

managementPearls.pearl: Do not forget to examine the contralateral side in a patient with strangulated groin hernia. Approximately 15-20% of patients will have a contralateral hernia that may also be at risk. In open repair, examine the other side while the patient is under anaesthesia. In laparoscopic repair (TAPP), the contralateral myopectineal orifice is visualized automatically.

Postoperative monitoring after strangulated hernia repair: serial lactates (every 6 hours for 24-48 hours), abdominal examination for peritonitis (tachycardia is often the first sign of an anastomotic leak), and monitoring for ongoing sepsis. An unexplained tachycardia >110 bpm in the first 48 hours is an anastomotic leak until proven otherwise — investigate aggressively.
managementPearls.evidence: Surgery textbook principles; WSES sepsis guidelines

managementPearls.pearl: In strangulated femoral hernia, the constriction is so tight that the bowel is often non-viable at the time of presentation. The low approach (groin incision) allows release of the constriction by incising the lacunar ligament, but if the bowel is non-viable, the approach is inadequate for a safe resection. Convert to a lower midline laparotomy or extend the groin incision into a laparotomy (connecting the groin to the midline).
managementPearls.evidence: McVay CB, Ann Surg 1966; Malek S et al., Hernia 2004

managementPearls.pearl: Always mark the hernia mass before attempting reduction or before the patient goes to theatre. If the hernia reduces spontaneously during anaesthesia induction (muscle relaxation), the mark tells you where the hernia was. A reduced strangulated hernia without a mark is a dangerous situation — you may not find the ischemic segment during laparoscopy.

The 'second-look' laparotomy is a critical strategy for strangulated hernia when bowel viability is uncertain after the first operation. Plan a second-look laparotomy 24-48 hours after the initial surgery. Close the abdomen temporarily (Bogota bag, vacuum pack, or simple towel clip closure). At second-look, reassess bowel viability. If viable, perform definitive anastomosis and hernia repair. If non-viable, resect additional segments.
managementPearls.evidence: Damage control surgery principles; African ICU study 2021

managementPearls.pearl: In strangulated hernia with extensive bowel necrosis, the decision to create a stoma versus performing a primary anastomosis depends on the '30-30-30' rule: if the patient is >30 minutes from the start of surgery, has received >30 ml/kg of fluid, or has a lactate >3.0 mmol/L — create a stoma rather than perform an anastomosis. Anastomotic leak rate in these patients exceeds 30%.

The 'vas deferens at risk' warning: during dissection of the hernia sac in a strangulated inguinal hernia, the vas deferens is tightly adherent to the edematous sac and may be difficult to identify. The vas is the whitest structure in the cord and has a characteristic 'washboard' feel (transversely oriented muscle fibers in its wall). Use a vessel loop to encircle and protect it before dissecting the sac. Injury to the vas causes obstructive azoospermia and is a significant cause of litigation.
managementPearls.evidence: Surgical anatomy textbooks; Medicolegal case series

operativeNuances.nuance: During groin exploration for strangulated inguinal hernia, make the incision large enough to allow adequate exposure. A standard oblique inguinal incision can be extended medially across the pubis (into a groin-to-midline extension) or laterally toward the anterior superior iliac spine if wider access is needed. Do not be afraid to extend the incision — inadequate exposure leads to inadequate assessment and unsafe surgery.
operativeNuances.evidence: Malek S et al., Hernia 2004; surgical technique textbooks

operativeNuances.nuance: When opening the hernia sac in a strangulated hernia, open it at the most lateral (or distal) aspect of the sac, away from the bowel. Use a scalpel to make a small nick, then extend with scissors under direct vision. Dark or feculent fluid under pressure will gush out — be prepared with suction. Note the colour and odour of the fluid: clear = early, serosanguinous = established, dark brown/feculent = gangrenous.
operativeNuances.evidence: Shouldice technique; Nyhus LM, Hernia repair textbooks

operativeNuances.nuance: After releasing the constriction ring, deliver the strangulated loop and place it on a warm saline-soaked swab. Observe for 10 minutes. The 'four signs of viability': (1) return of normal pink colour, (2) visible peristalsis, (3) palpable mesenteric pulsations, (4) bleeding when the serosa is nicked with a scalpel. If all four are present within 10 minutes, the bowel is viable. If not, resect.
operativeNuances.evidence: Bowel viability assessment; surgical physiology principles

operativeNuances.nuance: If you are performing a bowel resection through a groin incision, deliver the bowel ends through the wound and perform a hand-sewn end-to-end anastomosis with 3-0 absorbable sutures (single layer extramucosal). Ensure both ends are well-vascularized (bleeding from cut edge). After completing the anastomosis, check for a leak by gently squeezing the bowel on either side of the anastomosis — any leak should be reinforced.

In damage control situations for strangulated hernia, after resecting non-viable bowel, leave the bowel ends stapled or closed with a suture ligature. Do NOT attempt anastomosis. Mark the ends with a long suture to aid identification at second-look. Close the abdomen temporarily (vacuum pack or Bogota bag). At second-look (24-48h), assess the viability of the remaining bowel, then perform the anastomosis and hernia repair.
operativeNuances.evidence: Damage control surgery; African ICU study 2021

operativeNuances.nuance: When performing a primary tissue repair (Bassini/Shouldice) after resection for strangulated hernia, ensure the repair is tension-free. In edematous tissues, the Bassini repair may be under tension — consider using a relaxing incision (incision in the anterior rectus sheath) to reduce tension. If the tissues are too edematous or devitalized, simply close the subcutaneous tissue and skin and plan a delayed mesh repair in 6-12 weeks.

In damage control for strangulated hernia, the concept of 'abbreviated laparotomy' applies: limit the initial operation to (1) control of contamination — resect obviously non-viable bowel and close or staple the ends, (2) control of hemorrhage, (3) washout of the peritoneal cavity with warm saline, (4) temporary abdominal closure. Do NOT attempt definitive hernia repair or anastomosis during the index operation. The patient will return to theatre when physiologically stable (usually 24-48h).
operativeNuances.evidence: Damage control surgery; African ICU study 2021; WSES Guidelines 2022

operativeNuances.nuance: When assessing bowel viability during surgery for strangulated hernia, the use of intravenous fluorescein (10-15 mg/kg) with a Wood's lamp (ultraviolet light) can demonstrate perfused vs non-perfused bowel. The viable bowel fluoresces bright yellow-green within 30-60 seconds of injection; non-viable bowel shows no fluorescence. This technique is more accurate than clinical assessment alone and can help the surgeon decide where to transect the bowel.
operativeNuances.evidence: Fluorescein angiography in bowel ischemia; Marzella L et al., Surgery 1988

operativeNuances.nuance: After releasing the constriction ring in strangulated hernia, be prepared for 'reperfusion syndrome': as the ischemic bowel is reperfused, lactic acid and potassium are flushed from the venous drainage of the segment into the systemic circulation. This can cause transient hypotension, cardiac arrhythmias (especially if hyperkalemic), and worsening metabolic acidosis. The anaesthetist should be warned before the constriction is released. Prophylactic sodium bicarbonate (1 mEq/kg) may be considered.

In laparoscopic TAPP for strangulated hernia, establish pneumoperitoneum at 12 mmHg and inspect the hernia from the peritoneal side. The ischemic segment appears dusky or black and does not show peristalsis. If the bowel appears viable, reduce it gently and proceed with mesh repair. If non-viable, perform a laparoscopic-assisted resection through a small extension of a trocar site or convert to open. Do NOT attempt to close the peritoneum over a resected area — leave it open for drainage.
operativeNuances.evidence: Laparoscopic strangulated hernia repair; Bittner R et al., Hernia 2014

prognosis.naturalHistory: Strangulated hernia is a rapidly fatal condition if untreated. The timeline from onset of complete strangulation to death is typically 3-5 days: bowel necrosis within 6-12 hours, perforation within 12-24 hours, fecal peritonitis within 24-48 hours, and death from septic shock within 72-120 hours. Early surgery (within 6 hours of strangulation) can save the bowel in 80% of cases; each 6-hour delay beyond that reduces bowel salvage by 15%. In Kenya, the average time from symptom onset to surgery for strangulated hernia is 36-48 hours, meaning bowel salvage at surgery is less than 50%.
prognosis.recurrenceRates: After emergency repair with mesh (viable bowel, no contamination): 3-5% at 2 years. After primary tissue repair (non-viable bowel, contamination): 10-15% at 1 year. After damage control surgery with delayed repair: 15-20% overall (combining acute and elective repairs). The high recurrence rate after tissue repair reflects the poor tissue quality in strangulated cases.
prognosis.cancerRisk: A strangulated hernia may contain a colonic tumor that acted as a 'lead point' for the hernia (especially in the cecum or sigmoid colon). In patients >50 years undergoing bowel resection for strangulated hernia, the resected specimen should always be examined histologically to exclude an underlying malignancy. Colonoscopy is recommended 6-8 weeks after recovery if no malignancy was found in the resected specimen.
prognosis.qualityOfLife: Survivors of strangulated hernia with bowel resection have a significantly impaired quality of life for 3-6 months postoperatively. Persistent symptoms include: altered bowel habits (diarrhea or constipation in 20-30% of patients), chronic abdominal pain (10-15%), and incisional hernia at the laparotomy site (15-20%). Patients with stomas have the most severe quality of life impairment, especially in resource-limited settings where stoma care supplies are expensive and difficult to access. However, survival is the primary concern — most patients adapt to stomas and can undergo reversal within 3-6 months.
prognosis.mortality: Strangulated hernia with viable bowel (no resection): 2-5% mortality. Strangulated hernia with bowel resection: 10-15% mortality. Strangulated hernia with perforation and fecal peritonitis: 25-40% mortality. Strangulated hernia in elderly (>75 years) with comorbidities: 20-40% mortality. Overall mortality for strangulated hernia in high-income countries: 5-10%; in low-income countries including Kenya: 10-20%. The higher mortality reflects delayed presentation.
prognosis.diseaseModifyingFactors: Factors associated with worse prognosis: delayed presentation >24 hours, age >70 years, femoral hernia (vs inguinal), ASA III-IV, diabetes, hypertension, need for bowel resection, need for stoma, septic shock at presentation, lactate >4 mmol/L, and perforation with fecal peritonitis. Protective factors: early presentation (<12 hours), inguinal (vs femoral) hernia, viable bowel at surgery, ASA I-II, elective mesh repair (even after bowel resection, if clean-contaminated), and absence of comorbidities.

presentingComplaints: A known hernia that has become hard, painful, and cannot be pushed back in
Severe constant pain at the hernia site that is getting worse
Complete inability to pass stool or flatus
Fever with chills and feeling severely ill

severityAssessment.severityLabel: All strangulated hernias are SEVERE and require emergency surgery. Necrosis/peritonitis/shock are critical.

history_questions.question: When did the hernia become stuck and painful?
Was the pain initially cramping but now constant and severe?
When did you last pass stool or flatus?
Have you vomited? What colour — green, brown, or like stool?
Do you have fever or chills?
Is the skin over the hernia changing colour — red, purple, or black?
Do you feel dizzy, light-headed, or short of breath?
How long did you wait before coming to hospital?
Did you try any home remedies, herbs, or traditional medicine before coming?
Did you try to push the hernia back in yourself?
Do you have any known hernia for how long?
Have you had any hernia surgery before?
What type of hernia is it (groin, belly button, scar)?
Do you have diabetes, hypertension, or heart disease?
Are you on any blood thinners (aspirin, warfarin, clopidogrel)?
Do you smoke?
Have you been able to drink or eat anything?
How much urine have you passed in the last 12 hours?
Do you have any abdominal distension or swelling?
Is the pain becoming unbearable?
Have you tried any traditional treatment, herbal compresses, or manipulation at home?
Do you have any underlying chronic liver disease, TB, or HIV?
Have you had any previous abdominal surgeries (not hernia repairs)?
Was the hernia previously reducible and did you wear a truss or binder?
Have you had any episodes of fainting or near-fainting (suggesting septic shock)?
Is your abdomen becoming increasingly distended and tense?
Have you passed any blood from the rectum or seen blood on the toilet paper?
Do you feel confused, disoriented, or unusually sleepy (suggesting sepsis affecting the brain)?
Have you had any seizures or loss of consciousness?
Do you have any chronic kidney or liver disease?
Have you been on any antibiotics before coming to the hospital?
Do you have any allergies to medications, especially antibiotics?
When did you last eat a meal?
Do you have any recent travel history or exposure to contaminated food/water?
Have you been in contact with anyone who has similar symptoms or a diarrheal illness?
Do you have any known autoimmune disease or chronic inflammatory condition?
Have you ever had a colostomy or ileostomy that is now reversed?
Do you have any difficulty breathing or rapid breathing (suggesting metabolic acidosis)?
Have you noticed your urine output decreasing significantly?
Do you have any chest pain or palpitations?
For women: could you be pregnant? Is there any possibility of ectopic pregnancy?
Do you have any history of intra-abdominal or retroperitoneal surgery not related to the hernia?
Have you ever had a bowel perforation or peritonitis from any cause?

examination_findings.finding: Irreducible, tender, tense hernia mass — very painful to touch
Absent cough impulse — the hernia does not expand with coughing
Overlying skin changes: erythema, edema, or dusky discoloration
Skin gangrene or black discoloration in advanced cases
Signs of bowel obstruction: distension, tympanic percussion
Absent bowel sounds (late) or high-pitched (early)
Rebound tenderness or guarding if peritonitis developing
Tachycardia — often >110 bpm
Fever — high grade >38.5°C
Hypotension — indicating septic shock
Dehydration: dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes, reduced turgor
Confusion or altered mental status from sepsis
Oliguria from hypovolemia or sepsis
Tachypnea from metabolic acidosis or sepsis
Peritonitis — generalized tenderness, rigidity, guarding
Marked tenderness and edema extending beyond the hernia site into the surrounding tissues
Absent testicular reflex on the side of the strangulated inguinal hernia
Fluid thrill may be palpable over the hernia if the sac contains significant peritoneal fluid
Skin over the hernia may be cold to touch if the gangrenous segment is superficial and causing local tissue necrosis
The patient may be in the 'foetal position' — lying curled up with hips flexed to reduce tension on the abdominal wall and hernia site
Involuntary guarding and rigidity of the abdominal wall — a sign of generalized peritonitis from perforation of the strangulated segment
Loss of liver dullness on percussion — suggests free air from perforated viscus
Shallow, rapid breathing (Kussmaul respirations or tachypnea) from metabolic acidosis due to bowel ischemia
Cold, clammy peripheries with prolonged capillary refill (>3 seconds) indicating distributive shock from sepsis
Cyanosis or mottling of the skin around the hernia site in advanced gangrene
The hernia mass may feel 'stony hard' and immobile, with indistinct borders due to surrounding edema
Auscultation over the hernia: absence of bowel sounds (the trapped loop is no longer peristalsing due to ischemia)
Palpable crepitus extending from the hernia into the surrounding tissues (gas in soft tissues from gas-forming organisms)
The patient may have a 'toxic appearance' — anxious, agitated, or confused with sunken eyes and dry mucous membranes

In femoral hernia strangulation, the mass may be so small and deep that it is missed — examine the groin crease with the hip flexed, abducted, and externally rotated (frog-leg position) for better access to the femoral triangle

bedside_scores.name: Strangulation Severity Score (Emergency)
bedside_scores.interpretation: 0-5: early strangulation, emergency surgery with hope of viability; 6-10: established strangulation, likely bowel resection needed; 11-15: advanced with necrosis, high morbidity/mortality

subtypes.name: Strangulated Inguinal Hernia
subtypes.management: Emergency inguinal exploration. Reduce contents. Assess bowel viability. If viable: Lichtenstein mesh repair (if no contamination). If non-viable: open the internal ring, deliver the bowel, resect non-viable segment, perform anastomosis. Bassini/Shouldice repair (no mesh). Consider stoma if significant contamination.

subtypes.name: Strangulated Femoral Hernia
subtypes.management: Emergency exploration: open low approach (groin), preperitoneal approach (lower midline), or laparoscopic. Bowel resection if non-viable via separate laparotomy. Primary tissue repair (McVay). No mesh. Stoma if contamination.

subtypes.name: Strangulated Ventral/Incisional/Umbilical Hernia
subtypes.management: Emergency laparotomy or laparoscopy. Bowel resection as needed. Mesh repair only if no contamination. Primary closure if contaminated. Consider vacuum-assisted closure and delayed repair for severe contamination.

subtypes.name: Strangulated Richter's Hernia (Partial Enterocele)
subtypes.management: Emergency exploration. High suspicion in patients with tender groin mass without obstruction symptoms. Open the sac — inspect the bowel wall carefully for the trapped antimesenteric segment. Resect the involved segment (wedge resection or segmental bowel resection). Primary repair of the bowel wall. Hernia repair as per contamination status. Richter's is dangerous because it is easily missed.

bedside.name: Assessment of the hernia — reducibility, tenderness, cough impulse, skin changes
bedside.indication: diagnosis is clinical — do not delay surgery for imaging if classic signs present

bedside.name: NG tube insertion
bedside.indication: decompression of obstructed bowel, prevent aspiration

laboratory.name: Lactate
laboratory.indication: elevated in ischemia — >4 mmol/L strongly suggests bowel necrosis

laboratory.name: WBC count with differential
laboratory.indication: leukocytosis with left shift suggests strangulation

imaging.name: Groin/Abdominal Ultrasound with Doppler
imaging.indication: assess contents and vascularity IF immediately available; do NOT delay surgery

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis with IV contrast
imaging.indication: if diagnosis uncertain; can show bowel wall enhancement, mesenteric edema, free fluid

imaging.name: Abdominal X-Ray (erect)
imaging.indication: free air if perforated, air-fluid levels if obstructed

interpretation_rules.clinical_diagnosis_is_gold_standard: In a patient with known hernia, irreducible + tender + absent cough impulse + systemic toxicity = strangulated. Do not wait for imaging. Go to theatre.
interpretation_rules.lactate_and_viability: Lactate >4 mmol/L is highly suggestive of bowel ischemia. Falling lactate after resuscitation is reassuring; rising lactate despite fluids = ongoing ischemia = need for resection.
interpretation_rules.bowel_viability_assessment: Viable: pink, peristalsis, pulsatile mesenteric vessels. Non-viable: dusky/black, no peristalsis, no pulsations. If uncertain: warm saline packs for 5-10 minutes — repeat assessment.
interpretation_rules.reduction_en_masse_danger: NEVER force a hernia back in. Reduction en masse can push the entire hernia sac with non-viable bowel back into the abdomen — the obstruction persists but the hernia appears reduced. This is a surgical catastrophe.

management.protocolReference: World Society of Emergency Surgery (WSES) Guidelines for Strangulated Hernia (2022)
management.initial: THIS IS A SURGICAL EMERGENCY. Do not delay. IV fluids resuscitation (crystalloids). NBM, NG tube. IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (Piperacillin-Tazobactam or Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole ± Gentamicin). Catheterize. Correct electrolyte abnormalities. Blood transfusion if anemic. Informed consent for possible bowel resection and stoma. Prepare for ICU post-operatively.
management.definitive: Emergency surgery. Open approach (groin or midline laparotomy depending on hernia type). Reduce the contents carefully. Assess bowel viability. Viable: mesh repair (Lichtenstein/plug for inguinal, McVay for femoral). Non-viable: bowel resection + primary anastomosis. Primary tissue repair (Bassini/Shouldice). No mesh in contaminated cases. Stoma if extensive resection or significant contamination. Laparoscopic approach only if surgeon experienced.
management.postop: ICU admission if septic or bowel resection. Continue IV antibiotics. NG tube until bowel function returns. VTE prophylaxis. Monitor for ongoing sepsis, anastomotic leak. Wound care. Stoma care if created. Consider second-look laparotomy if bowel viability uncertain.

pathways.criteria: irreducible tender mass, no generalized peritonitis, early presentation (<12h), lactate <4
pathways.treatment: Emergency surgery. Open exploration. Assess bowel viability. Warm saline packs. If viable: mesh repair. If non-viable: resect and primary tissue repair.

pathways.criteria: generalized peritonitis, septic shock, bowel obstruction with necrosis, lactate >4, duration >12h
pathways.treatment: Emergency laparotomy. Resuscitation. Bowel resection. Primary anastomosis or stoma depending on contamination. Primary tissue repair of hernia (NO mesh). Abdominal closure (or VAC therapy). ICU. Vasopressors.

operative_templates.procedure: Emergency Exploration of Strangulated Inguinal Hernia
operative_templates.incision: Oblique groin incision, extended as needed
steps: Oblique groin incision over the inguinal canal
Identify the spermatic cord and hernia sac
Open the hernia sac laterally to avoid bowel injury
Note the colour of the peritoneal fluid (clear = viable, bloody = necrotic)
Release the constriction — incise the external ring (or internal ring if indirect)
Deliver the bowel loop — use warm saline packs
Assess viability: colour, peristalsis, mesenteric pulsations
If viable: reduce, proceed with Lichtenstein mesh repair
If non-viable: open the posterior wall of the inguinal canal, incise the internal ring, deliver more bowel
Clamp the bowel proximal and distal to the non-viable segment
Resect the non-viable segment (if limited, through the groin; if extensive, via separate lower midline laparotomy)
Return the anastomosed bowel to the abdomen
Repair the posterior wall with Bassini or Shouldice (NO MESH)
Place a drain if contamination present
Close external oblique, subcutaneous tissue, skin (or leave open if severely contaminated)

dischargeCriteria: Wound clean and dry (or manageable wound care)
Full discussion of histology results (if bowel resected)

patient_education.explanation: A strangulated hernia occurs when the blood supply to the bowel trapped in a hernia is cut off. This is a life-threatening emergency — the bowel will die within hours if not treated. The dead bowel must be cut out, and the hernia opening must be repaired. In Kenya, many patients come late because they try traditional remedies first. Do NOT wait if your hernia becomes painful, hard, and cannot be pushed back. Go directly to a hospital with surgical capabilities.
patient_education.warningSigns: Return to hospital IMMEDIATELY if you had a hernia: it becomes hard, tender, and irreducible, you have constant severe pain, you vomit repeatedly or vomit brown fluid, you have fever with chills, your abdomen becomes distended, or you cannot pass stool or gas.
patient_education.activity: If you had a bowel resection, recovery takes longer. No heavy lifting for 6-8 weeks. Walking is encouraged. If you have a stoma, you will receive special instructions. Return to work depends on the extent of surgery: desk work 2-4 weeks, manual labour 8-12 weeks.
patient_education.diet: Start with clear liquids. Progress to soft bland diet. Eat small frequent meals. If you had a bowel resection, you may need a low-residue diet initially. Use stool softeners. If you have a stoma, you will receive dietary counseling — avoid foods that block (nuts, popcorn, maize seeds).
patient_education.medication: Take all prescribed antibiotics (full course even if feeling better). Take pain medication as prescribed. If you had a stoma, clean the bag and skin daily with mild soap and water. Do not take traditional herbal remedies without consulting your doctor.
patient_education.followUp: You will need close follow-up. Return in 1-2 weeks for wound and stoma check. If you had a stoma, reversal is usually planned in 3-6 months. Discuss this with your surgeon before discharge.

followup.before_discharge: Ensure hemodynamic stability. Wound assessment. Stoma care teaching (if applicable). Histology review if bowel resected. Nutritional assessment. Discuss stoma reversal timeline. Psychological support if needed.

after_discharge.1_week: Wound check, drain removal. Stoma assessment. Review histology. Pain control review.
after_discharge.2_weeks: Wound healing assessment. Stoma therapy follow-up. Plan stoma reversal timing. Check for recurrence.
after_discharge.6_weeks: Full recovery evaluation. Discuss stoma reversal (3-6 months). Assess for incisional hernia. Nutritional optimization. Smoking cessation.
after_discharge.3_months: Schedule stoma reversal surgery (if applicable). Final check for recurrence. Chronic pain assessment.
after_discharge.6_months: Follow-up after stoma reversal. Incisional hernia surveillance. Long-term bowel function assessment.

followup.when_to_return: Immediately if: severe abdominal pain, fever, vomiting, wound discharge or dehiscence, stoma not functioning (no output >8h), dark/bloody stoma output, dehydration with dizziness/weakness, or recurrent irreducible hernia.
